intent: Check whether the candidate has a practical mental model for the five SOLID principles rather than only memorizing names.
primaryQuestion: What does SOLID stand for? Briefly explain each principle in practical terms.

expectedSignals.label: Names all five SOLID principles correctly.

requirements.description: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, and Dependency Inversion are identified.
Avoids replacing principle names with unrelated design patterns.

expectedSignals.label: Explains each principle as a design pressure, not a rigid rule.

requirements.description: SRP is about one reason to change, not one method.
OCP is about extension without risky modification.
LSP is about substitutability.
ISP is about small client-focused interfaces.
DIP is about high-level policy depending on abstractions.

expectedSignals.label: Connects principles to maintainability and testing.

requirements.description: Mentions reducing coupling and increasing cohesion.
Explains how abstractions and smaller responsibilities make unit tests easier.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that over-applying SOLID can overcomplicate small code.

requirements.description: Mentions balancing direct code with future change pressure.
Avoids creating abstractions before there is a real variation point.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Pick one SOLID principle and explain a bug or refactor it would help with.
followUps.purpose: Move from acronym recall to practical use.

followUps.question: When would adding an interface for every class be overengineering?

en.title: SOLID principles overview
en.displayQuestion: What does SOLID stand for? Briefly explain each principle in practical terms.
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate has a practical mental model for the five SOLID principles rather than only memorizing names.
guidance: Start with the core mechanism, not only a definition.
Use a concrete backend or frontend example where the concept changes behavior.
Name one common mistake or production consequence.
commonMistakes: Answering only with memorized keywords.
Ignoring boundary cases or failure modes.
Not connecting the concept to real implementation decisions.

vi.title: Tổng quan SOLID principles
vi.displayQuestion: SOLID là viết tắt của gì? Hãy giải thích ngắn gọn từng principle theo hướng thực tế.
vi.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate has a practical mental model for the five SOLID principles rather than only memorizing names.
guidance: Bắt đầu bằng cơ chế cốt lõi, không chỉ đọc định nghĩa.
Dùng một ví dụ backend hoặc frontend cụ thể nơi khái niệm này làm thay đổi hành vi.
Nêu một lỗi phổ biến hoặc hệ quả trong production.
commonMistakes: Chỉ trả lời bằng keyword học thuộc.
Bỏ qua boundary case hoặc failure mode.
Không kết nối khái niệm với quyết định implement thực tế.

ja.title: SOLID principles overview
ja.displayQuestion: 次の点を説明してください: What does SOLID stand for? Briefly explain each principle in practical terms.
ja.displayIntent: この質問は solid_overview を実務で説明できるかを確認します。
guidance: 定義だけでなく、中心となる仕組みから説明する。
その概念が挙動を変える具体的な backend/frontend の例を使う。
よくある mistake または production consequence を一つ述べる。
commonMistakes: 暗記した keyword だけで答える。
boundary case や failure mode を無視する。
概念を実装上の判断につなげない。

intent: Assess whether the candidate can identify too many reasons to change in a service and split responsibilities without arbitrary fragmentation.
primaryQuestion: Explain SRP with a concrete backend example such as a UserService doing creation, password reset, email sending, and profile updates.

expectedSignals.label: Defines SRP as one reason to change.

requirements.description: Explains reason-to-change, not one method or one tiny class.
Connects responsibility to business capability or actor-driven change.

expectedSignals.label: Identifies mixed responsibilities in the UserService example.

requirements.description: Separates account creation/profile logic from password reset flow.
Separates email notification infrastructure from core user domain logic.

expectedSignals.label: Proposes a practical refactor.

requirements.description: Suggests focused services such as UserService, PasswordResetService, NotificationService, or EmailService.
Keeps orchestration clear without scattering one use case across too many files.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions testing and change impact benefits.

requirements.description: Smaller services are easier to test with mocks.
Changing email provider should not force changes in user creation rules.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Why does SRP not mean a class can only have one method?
Which tests become easier after separating email sending from user creation?

intent: Check whether the candidate understands extension through stable abstractions and avoids endless if/else modification when variation points are real.
primaryQuestion: Explain OCP. How would you add a new payment method without modifying existing payment logic?

expectedSignals.label: Defines OCP as open for extension and closed for risky modification.

requirements.description: New behavior can be added with new implementations.
Existing tested behavior should not need repeated edits for each new variant.

expectedSignals.label: Uses a strategy-like abstraction for payment providers.

requirements.description: Defines a common PaymentProvider or PaymentStrategy contract.
Adds Stripe, Momo, or VNPay as separate implementations.

expectedSignals.label: Explains registration or selection of implementations.

requirements.description: Mentions provider registry, factory, dependency injection, or configuration-based selection.
Avoids hard-coded long if/else branches in the central service.

expectedSignals.label: States when OCP can be premature.

requirements.description: Avoids abstracting before there are multiple real payment variants.
Balances indirection cost against expected change.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: What interface would every payment provider need to implement?
If the app has only one payment provider and no roadmap for more, would you still add a strategy layer?

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands substitutability through contracts, not just inheritance syntax.
primaryQuestion: What is LSP? Give an example where substituting a subclass breaks the system.

expectedSignals.label: Defines LSP as safe substitutability of subtype for base type.

requirements.description: Code using the base type should still behave correctly with any subtype.
The subtype must honor the expectations of the base contract.

expectedSignals.label: Discusses method inputs, outputs, errors, and side effects.

requirements.description: Subtype should not require stricter preconditions than the base contract.
Subtype should not return incompatible results or throw surprising unsupported errors.

expectedSignals.label: Gives a concrete violation example.

requirements.description: Uses examples such as Square/Rectangle, read-only repository extending writable repository, or bird/flying bird.
Explains exactly what caller assumption breaks.

expectedSignals.label: Suggests a better model.

requirements.description: Uses composition or smaller interfaces when inheritance does not express a true is-a relationship.
Avoids inheritance solely for code reuse.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Why is throwing UnsupportedOperationException from a subclass method often an LSP smell?
How would you redesign the hierarchy if not all birds can fly?

intent: Check whether the candidate can split interfaces around client needs to reduce forced dependencies and fake implementations.
primaryQuestion: Explain ISP. What problem does it solve compared with one large god interface?

expectedSignals.label: Defines ISP as clients should not depend on methods they do not use.

requirements.description: Interfaces should be shaped around consumers.
Large interfaces force implementations and callers to know irrelevant methods.

expectedSignals.label: Identifies god interface symptoms.

requirements.description: Implementations throw not supported for methods they do not actually support.
Mocks and tests require unrelated methods.

expectedSignals.label: Proposes a practical split.

requirements.description: Splits read, write, search, import, or export capabilities into smaller interfaces.
Composes capabilities where a class truly supports multiple roles.

expectedSignals.label: Connects ISP to easier testing and safer change.

requirements.description: Changing export behavior should not impact read-only consumers.
Small interfaces reduce mock setup and accidental coupling.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: How would you split a Repository interface with read, write, search, import, and export methods?
Why do fake methods that throw not supported indicate a design smell?

intent: Check whether the candidate separates the design principle from the wiring technique and understands why high-level policy should not depend directly on infrastructure details.
primaryQuestion: What is DIP, and how is it different from Dependency Injection?

expectedSignals.label: Defines DIP as depending on abstractions rather than concrete low-level details.

requirements.description: High-level business logic should not be tightly coupled to concrete infrastructure classes.
Abstractions should represent stable business needs or ports.

expectedSignals.label: Defines DI as a technique for supplying dependencies from the outside.

requirements.description: Constructor injection or a DI container can provide dependencies.
DI can help implement DIP but does not automatically guarantee good abstractions.

expectedSignals.label: Gives a backend example.

requirements.description: Service depends on EmailSender interface or token instead of directly constructing SendGrid client.
Repository or gateway implementation can be replaced in tests.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions testing and configuration benefits.

requirements.description: Mocks or fakes can replace infrastructure in unit tests.
Different implementations can be selected by environment or module configuration.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Can code use dependency injection and still violate DIP?
In NestJS, why might an injection token be useful for a payment gateway?
followUps.purpose: Connect DIP to Nest provider design.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can use SOLID vocabulary to reason about why code is hard or easy to change.
primaryQuestion: How do cohesion and coupling relate to SOLID principles?

expectedSignals.label: Defines cohesion as related responsibilities belonging together.

requirements.description: High cohesion means a module or class has focused, related behavior.
Low cohesion appears when unrelated workflows live in the same service.

expectedSignals.label: Defines coupling as dependency between modules.

requirements.description: High coupling means one module changes or breaks when another changes.
Low coupling means implementation details are hidden behind stable contracts.

expectedSignals.label: Maps SOLID principles to cohesion and coupling.

requirements.description: SRP and ISP tend to improve cohesion.
DIP and OCP tend to reduce coupling to concrete details.
LSP protects coupling through reliable contracts.

expectedSignals.label: Uses code review symptoms.

requirements.description: Too many dependencies, unrelated method groups, circular imports, or fragile tests can indicate low cohesion or high coupling.
Explains that metrics are clues, not automatic proof.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: What would low cohesion look like in an OrderService?
Can low coupling ever make code harder to understand?

intent: Check whether the candidate can recognize god service and orchestration smells without mechanically counting constructor parameters.
primaryQuestion: A service has many injected dependencies. What design smell might it indicate, and how would you split it?

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes possible god service or mixed responsibility smell.

requirements.description: Many dependencies may mean the service coordinates too many use cases.
Does not claim dependency count alone proves a violation.

expectedSignals.label: Groups dependencies by use case or capability.

requirements.description: Separates payment, notification, inventory, user, or reporting responsibilities.
Keeps a thin orchestrator only when a workflow genuinely spans multiple services.

expectedSignals.label: Refactors around cohesive services.

requirements.description: Extracts focused services or domain components with clear ownership.
Avoids moving methods randomly just to reduce constructor length.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions tests and change impact.

requirements.description: Unit tests become smaller and easier to mock.
Changes in one dependency area affect fewer workflows.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: What questions would you ask before splitting a service with ten injected dependencies?
How would tests reveal that a service has too many responsibilities?

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can decide when a strategy map or polymorphism improves maintainability and when a switch is still acceptable.
primaryQuestion: How would you refactor a long switch statement that chooses behavior based on type?

expectedSignals.label: Identifies why long conditional behavior selection can violate OCP.

requirements.description: Adding each new type requires editing the central switch.
The switch may become a hotspot for unrelated behaviors.

expectedSignals.label: Suggests strategy, handler map, or polymorphic dispatch.

requirements.description: Defines a common interface for behavior handlers.
Registers handlers by type in a map, factory, or DI container.

expectedSignals.label: Preserves validation and unknown type handling.

requirements.description: Handles unsupported type explicitly.
Keeps type-to-handler mapping testable and discoverable.

expectedSignals.label: States when a switch is acceptable.

requirements.description: A small stable switch over simple values can be clearer than premature strategy abstraction.
Refactor pressure increases when behavior grows or changes often.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate treats SOLID as judgment-based guidance rather than ceremony.
primaryQuestion: When can applying OCP or SOLID too early lead to overengineering, and how would you decide the right balance?

expectedSignals.label: Explains how premature abstraction adds cost.

requirements.description: Extra interfaces, factories, and indirection make simple flows harder to read.
Unused variation points can slow delivery and onboarding.

expectedSignals.label: Uses change likelihood as a decision input.

requirements.description: Abstracts when multiple implementations exist or change is likely.
Keeps direct code when requirements are simple and stable.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions refactoring as a valid path.

requirements.description: Starts simple with tests and extracts abstractions when duplication or change pressure appears.
Avoids both reckless coupling and speculative architecture.

expectedSignals.label: Communicates trade-offs in code review.

requirements.description: Explains why a chosen design is enough for the current risk.
Leaves clear extension points only where justified.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can connect design principles to concrete test friction and isolation.
primaryQuestion: How do SOLID principles improve unit testing and mocking?

expectedSignals.label: Connects SRP to smaller test scope.

requirements.description: Focused classes need fewer test scenarios per unit.
Unrelated behavior does not require setup for every test.

expectedSignals.label: Connects DIP to replaceable dependencies.

requirements.description: Business logic can depend on abstractions that are easy to fake or mock.
External services, databases, and queues can be replaced in unit tests.

expectedSignals.label: Connects ISP to smaller mocks.

requirements.description: Tests do not need fake methods unrelated to the use case.
Narrow interfaces reduce accidental coupling in test setup.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions testability is a signal but not the only goal.

requirements.description: Hard-to-test code can reveal poor boundaries.
Tests should not force artificial abstractions with no design value.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate can spot responsibility mixing in data models and avoid placing infrastructure behavior in the wrong layer.
primaryQuestion: Which SOLID principle is violated when a DTO, entity, or domain model contains unrelated validation, persistence, and email-sending logic?

expectedSignals.label: Identifies SRP as the main violation.

requirements.description: The model has multiple unrelated reasons to change.
Validation rules, persistence mapping, and email infrastructure evolve for different reasons.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions possible layering or coupling problems.

requirements.description: Email sending inside an entity couples domain/data shape to infrastructure.
Persistence concerns can leak into API DTOs or domain behavior.

expectedSignals.label: Suggests appropriate placement of responsibilities.

requirements.description: DTOs define transport shape and request validation boundaries.
Entities or domain models preserve invariants.
Services or adapters handle persistence and notifications.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids anemic-domain absolutism.

requirements.description: Recognizes domain models can contain behavior related to their invariants.
Separates related domain behavior from unrelated infrastructure side effects.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate sees patterns as tools for design forces rather than names to memorize.
primaryQuestion: How are SOLID principles related to patterns like Strategy, Factory, Adapter, and Decorator?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that patterns are implementation tools, not goals by themselves.

requirements.description: Patterns can help satisfy SOLID pressures.
Using a pattern without a real problem can add unnecessary complexity.

expectedSignals.label: Maps Strategy to OCP and DIP.

requirements.description: New strategies extend behavior without editing central logic.
Business logic can depend on a strategy abstraction.

expectedSignals.label: Maps Adapter and Decorator to coupling and extension.

requirements.description: Adapter hides incompatible external interfaces behind a local contract.
Decorator adds behavior around an object without changing its core implementation.

expectedSignals.label: Maps Factory to creation responsibility.

requirements.description: Factory can centralize complex object creation.
Factory can prevent constructors or services from knowing too much about concrete implementations.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can decompose a realistic feature into cohesive responsibilities and abstractions.
primaryQuestion: How would you apply SOLID when designing a file upload feature with validation, storage, virus scanning, and metadata persistence?

expectedSignals.label: Separates feature responsibilities coherently.

requirements.description: Validation, storage, scanning, metadata persistence, and orchestration are distinct responsibilities.
The upload flow remains understandable as one use case.

expectedSignals.label: Defines useful abstractions for external dependencies.

requirements.description: Storage can be behind FileStorage interface or token.
Virus scanning can be behind Scanner or SecurityCheck abstraction.

expectedSignals.label: Keeps domain and infrastructure boundaries clear.

requirements.description: Metadata persistence belongs in repository or persistence adapter.
Business orchestration should not depend on concrete S3, local disk, or scanner SDK details.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions failure handling and tests.

requirements.description: Defines what happens if scan fails after upload succeeds.
Mocks storage and scanner for unit tests and covers integration boundaries separately.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Which class or service would own the decision to reject a file because the scan failed?
What cleanup is needed if storage succeeds but metadata persistence fails?

intent: Check whether the candidate can use design-smell signals pragmatically during review without making premature accusations.
primaryQuestion: In a code review, what signs would make you suspect SOLID violations before reading every implementation detail?

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes size and responsibility smells.

requirements.description: Very large services, unrelated method groups, or many reasons to change suggest SRP issues.
Large interfaces with unused methods suggest ISP issues.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes coupling and dependency smells.

requirements.description: Direct construction of infrastructure inside business logic suggests DIP issues.
Circular dependencies or many injected services may suggest boundary problems.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes behavioral extension smells.

requirements.description: Long repeated if/else or switch blocks for type behavior may suggest OCP issues.
Subclasses throwing unsupported errors may suggest LSP issues.

expectedSignals.label: Uses review communication carefully.

requirements.description: Treats smells as investigation prompts, not proof.
Asks for examples, tests, and expected variation before requesting abstraction.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Which SOLID smell might be suggested by a service constructor with twelve dependencies?
How would you phrase a review comment without sounding dogmatic?

en.title: Spotting SOLID smells in code review
en.displayQuestion: In a code review, what signs would make you suspect SOLID violations before reading every implementation detail?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can use design-smell signals pragmatically during review without making premature accusations.
guidance: Start with the core mechanism, not only a definition.
Use a concrete backend or frontend example where the concept changes behavior.
Name one common mistake or production consequence.
commonMistakes: Answering only with memorized keywords.
Ignoring boundary cases or failure modes.
Not connecting the concept to real implementation decisions.

vi.title: Nhận biết SOLID smell trong code review
vi.displayQuestion: Trong code review, dấu hiệu nào khiến bạn nghi ngờ có SOLID violation trước khi đọc hết implementation?
vi.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can use design-smell signals pragmatically during review without making premature accusations.
guidance: Bắt đầu bằng cơ chế cốt lõi, không chỉ đọc định nghĩa.
Dùng một ví dụ backend hoặc frontend cụ thể nơi khái niệm này làm thay đổi hành vi.
Nêu một lỗi phổ biến hoặc hệ quả trong production.
commonMistakes: Chỉ trả lời bằng keyword học thuộc.
Bỏ qua boundary case hoặc failure mode.
Không kết nối khái niệm với quyết định implement thực tế.

ja.title: Spotting SOLID smells in code review
ja.displayQuestion: 次の点を説明してください: In a code review, what signs would make you suspect SOLID violations before reading every implementation detail?
ja.displayIntent: この質問は code_review を実務で説明できるかを確認します。
guidance: 定義だけでなく、中心となる仕組みから説明する。
その概念が挙動を変える具体的な backend/frontend の例を使う。
よくある mistake または production consequence を一つ述べる。
commonMistakes: 暗記した keyword だけで答える。
boundary case や failure mode を無視する。
概念を実装上の判断につなげない。

sourceReferences.label: Martin Fowler: Design Principles and Patterns
sourceReferences.note: Used for design principle and refactoring vocabulary.

sourceReferences.label: Microsoft Learn: SOLID principles
sourceReferences.note: Used for mainstream SOLID principle framing.

sourceReferences.label: Robert C. Martin SOLID principle origin
sourceReferences.note: Used as historical background for the SOLID principle family.
